I INTRODUCTION 
I.1 Product name  
OMI_EXTREME_SL_BALTIC_slev_mean_and_anomaly_obs 
OMI_EXTREME_SL_NORTHWESTSHELF_slev_mean_and_anomaly_obs 
OMI_EXTREME_SL_IBI_slev_mean_and_anomaly_obs 
OMI_EXTREME_SL_MEDSEA_slev_mean_and_anomaly_obs 
I.2 Indicator evaluation method 
The EXTREME SL indicators are based on the computation of the 99th and the 
1st percentiles (that represent approximately the 1% highest and lowest values, 
respectively) of  hourly sea levels derived from in situ sea level observations , 
following the same approach applied by Woodworth and Blackman (2004) to 
extreme sea levels. This me thod determines changes in the frequency 
distribution of the measured variables. 
All the data used for the computation of these indicators have been 
downloaded from Copernicus Marine Service In Situ Thematic Assembly Centre 
(In Situ TAC ) products. The analysis is performed for the following variable: 
coastal sea level (slev, total sea level at each tide gauge, including the tide) 
measured by in situ tide gauges (hourly values).  
The periods selected are based on our analysis of the data available in the  
different In Situ TAC  regions that fulfilled specific criteria of data completeness 
and quality (Pérez Gómez et al 2016, Pérez Gómez et al 2018). Tide gauges 
were selected according to consolidated and reprocessed  time series of 
hourly sea level data in Copernicus Marine Service  for the period 1993 to the 
target year, product INSITU_GLO_PHY_SSH_DISCRETE_MY_013_053. Within these 
periods, a minimum number of 15 years with completeness index larger than 
70% is required for a station to be included. These data  were then used to 
compute annually the 99th and 1st percentile levels at each station. 
More details can be found in the Copernicus Marine Service  Ocean State 
Report (Pérez Gómez et al., 2016; Pérez Gómez et al., 2018).
